edges.description: CSB-deficient iPSC-derived neural models exhibit impaired neural progenitor migration via defective autophagy, linking the upstream TC-NER defect to clinical microcephaly.
CSB deficiency impairs oligodendrocyte maturation in iPSC-derived neural systems, providing a downstream cellular consequence of the underlying TC-NER defect that explains CNS demyelination.
CSB deficiency in iPSC-derived neural models produces abnormal GABA neurotransmitter levels suggestive of a disturbed developmental GABA switch downstream of the underlying TC-NER defect.
CS fibroblasts show repression of the NRF2 oxidative-stress response pathway, identifying redox dysregulation as a downstream consequence of impaired TC-NER and DNA-repair-coupled transcription.
CS fibroblasts show inflammatory activation of arachidonic acid metabolism downstream of impaired DNA-repair-coupled transcription.
Loss of CSA/CSB function leads to depletion of mitochondrial DNA polymerase gamma (POLG1), placing mitochondrial DNA maintenance deficiency downstream of the upstream TC-NER/transcription defect.

CS fibroblasts develop a progeroid-specific DNA methylation signature that is absent in UV-sensitive syndrome (which shares the NER defect but lacks progeria), implicating CSA/CSB-dependent transcription functions as the upstream driver of accelerated epigenomic aging.
The CS TC-NER defect renders long-lived myelinating Schwann cells vulnerable to unrepaired transcription-blocking DNA lesions, producing the characteristic demyelinating polyneuropathy phenotype.
Failure to remove transcription-blocking UV-induced lesions directly explains photosensitive skin injury in Cockayne syndrome.
The cited CSB neural-model study identifies impaired neural progenitor migration as an explanation for clinical microcephaly.
The disturbed developmental GABA switch impairs brain circuit formation in CSB-deficient iPSC-derived neural models, directly producing altered neuronal network functionality.
Disturbed GABA-switch timing is linked in CSB neural models to impaired brain circuit formation and intellectual disability.
NRF2 pathway repression compromises antioxidant defenses and links oxidative stress to accelerated cellular senescence in CS fibroblasts.
Arachidonic acid metabolism activation drives chronic inflammatory signaling that contributes to accelerated cellular senescence in CS fibroblasts.
POLG1 depletion compromises mitochondrial DNA maintenance and contributes to the broader mitochondrial dysfunction and impaired mitophagy phenotype seen in CS cells and tissues.
Mitochondrial dysfunction and impaired mitophagy drive accumulation of damaged mitochondria, oxidative stress, and energetic failure that converge on accelerated cellular senescence in CS.
The CS-specific progeroid epigenomic signature (advanced epigenetic age, reprogramming of developmental and synaptic gene programs) provides a molecular substrate for accelerated cellular senescence.

nodes.description: Cockayne syndrome is a segmental progeroid syndrome in which defective DNA repair leads to accelerated cellular aging. Oxidative stress and autophagy dysfunction contribute to cellular senescence observed across multiple organ systems.

evidence.reference_title: Supplementation with nicotinamide limits accelerated aging in affected individuals with cockayne syndrome and restores antioxidant defenses.
evidence.snippet: Cockayne syndrome (CS) is a segmental progeroid syndrome characterized by defects in the DNA excision repair pathway, predisposing to neurodegenerative manifestations.

evidence.reference_title: Supplementation with nicotinamide limits accelerated aging in affected individuals with cockayne syndrome and restores antioxidant defenses.
evidence.snippet: This study reveals the link between oxidative stress and accelerated aging in affected individuals with CS and highlights new biomarkers of cellular senescence.

nodes.description: Cockayne syndrome fibroblasts show activation of arachidonic acid metabolism, contributing to inflammatory dysregulation. This abnormality is partially reversible with nicotinamide supplementation.

evidence.reference_title: Supplementation with nicotinamide limits accelerated aging in affected individuals with cockayne syndrome and restores antioxidant defenses.
evidence.snippet: Gene expression analysis revealed alterations in two main pathways. This involves the activation of arachidonic acid metabolism and the repression of the NRF2 pathway in affected individuals with CS.

evidence.reference_title: Supplementation with nicotinamide limits accelerated aging in affected individuals with cockayne syndrome and restores antioxidant defenses.
evidence.snippet: The supplementation with nicotinamide adjusted these abnormalities by enhancing autophagy and decreasing inflammation.

nodes.description: Pathogenic ERCC6/ERCC8 variants impair transcription-coupled nucleotide excision repair (TC-NER), disrupting removal of transcription-blocking DNA lesions in actively transcribed genes. TC-NER is initiated by stalling of elongating RNA polymerase complexes at damaged sites.

evidence.reference_title: Transcription-Coupled Nucleotide Excision Repair: A Faster Solution or the Only Option?
evidence.snippet: A branch of the nucleotide excision repair (NER) pathway, transcription-coupled repair (TCR or TC-NER) specifically operates on the template DNA strand of actively transcribed genes.

evidence.reference_title: Transcription-Coupled Repair and R-Loop Crosstalk in Genome Stability.
evidence.snippet: TCR, a specialized sub-pathway of nucleotide excision repair, rapidly removes transcription-blocking lesions from the transcribed strand of active genes, thereby safeguarding transcription fidelity and cellular homeostasis.

evidence.reference_title: Spectrum of ERCC6-Related Cockayne Syndrome (Type B): From Mild to Severe Forms.
evidence.snippet: (1) Background: Cockayne syndrome (CS) is an ultra-rare multisystem disorder, classically subdivided into three forms and characterized by a clinical spectrum without a clear genotype-phenotype correlation for both the two causative genes ERCC6 (CS type B) and ERCC8 (CS type A).

evidence.snippet: altered neuronal network functionality and neurotransmitter GABA levels, which is suggestive of a disturbed GABA switch that likely impairs brain circuit formation and ultimately causes intellectual disability

nodes.description: Genome-wide DNA methylation profiling distinguishes Cockayne syndrome fibroblasts from those of UV-sensitive syndrome (which lacks the progeroid phenotype) and identifies a CS-specific epigenomic signature associated with accelerated biological age. The CS-specific differentially methylated genes are enriched in developmental transcription factors and synaptic neurodevelopmental programs.

evidence.reference_title: Epigenomic signature of accelerated ageing in progeroid Cockayne syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Differential analysis highlighted a CS-specific epigenomic signature (progeroid-related; not present in UVSS) enriched in three categories: developmental transcription factors, ion/neurotransmitter membrane transporters and synaptic neuro-developmental genes.

evidence.reference_title: Epigenomic signature of accelerated ageing in progeroid Cockayne syndrome.
evidence.snippet: the epigenetic clock returned a marked increase in CS biological age respect to healthy and UVSS cells.

nodes.description: CS proteins participate in mitochondrial homeostasis. CSA/CSB deficiency leads to depletion of mitochondrial DNA polymerase gamma (POLG1), which can be restored by nicotinamide supplementation, linking nuclear DNA repair defects to mitochondrial dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Supplementation with nicotinamide limits accelerated aging in affected individuals with cockayne syndrome and restores antioxidant defenses.
evidence.snippet: CSA/CSB-dependent depletion of the mitochondrial DNA polymerase-γ catalytic subunit (POLG1) was restored following nicotinamide supplementation in CS-affected individuals' fibroblasts.

nodes.description: Cross-species transcriptomic and biochemical studies demonstrate that Cockayne syndrome involves mitochondrial dysfunction and compromised mitophagy/autophagy, contributing to accelerated cellular aging. Restoration of NAD+ levels through precursor supplementation rescues mitochondrial dysfunction in CS cellular and nematode models, identifying NAD+ signaling as a convergent target connecting DNA damage accumulation with mitochondrial failure.

evidence.reference_title: Cockayne syndrome proteins CSA and CSB maintain mitochondrial homeostasis through NAD(+) signaling.
evidence.snippet: Our cross-species transcriptomic analysis in CS postmortem brain tissue, CS mouse, and nematode models shows that mitochondrial dysfunction is indeed a common feature in CS.

evidence.reference_title: Cockayne syndrome proteins CSA and CSB maintain mitochondrial homeostasis through NAD(+) signaling.
evidence.snippet: In primary cells depleted for CSA or CSB, this dysfunction can be corrected with supplementation of NAD+ precursors.

nodes.description: Peripheral nerve involvement is a recognized component of Cockayne syndrome, particularly demyelinating polyneuropathy, reflecting vulnerability of long-lived myelinating Schwann cells to unrepaired transcription-blocking DNA lesions.

evidence.reference_title: Peripheral neuropathies associated with DNA repair disorders.
evidence.snippet: Cockayne syndrome has classically been linked to demyelinating polyneuropathies, whereas xeroderma pigmentosum has long been associated with axonal polyneuropathies.
